item.title: “I do not see any other form, monks, that so obsesses and occupies the mind of a woman as the form of a man. The…
item.description:
“I do not see any other form, monks, that so obsesses and occupies the mind of a woman as the form of a man. The form of a man, monks, obsesses and occupies the mind of a woman.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 1.6
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Again, the five kinds of kings Are present in the world — Learn to subdue them, Always keeping mindfulness. Toward forms and sounds, Toward tastes…
item.description:
“Again, the five kinds of kings
Are present in the world —
Learn to subdue them,
Always keeping mindfulness.
Toward forms and sounds,
Toward tastes and smells,
And toward contacts —
Subdue desire and lust.

Subdue desire
For these things.
The mindful monk,
With mind well liberated,
Then, at the right time,
Rightly reflecting on the Dhamma,
With unified mind,
He should destroy darkness.” So spoke the Blessed One.

From: Sutta Nipāta 4.16
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Raṭṭhapāla: Behold this decorated image — A heap of wounds, a collection of sores, Diseased, full of lustful thoughts, Nothing stable or permanent here.…
item.description:
Elder Raṭṭhapāla:

Behold this decorated image —
A heap of wounds, a collection of sores,
Diseased, full of lustful thoughts,
Nothing stable or permanent here.

Behold this adorned appearance,
With jewels and earrings —
A skeleton covered by skin,
Made beautiful by clothes.

The feet are painted with lac and dye,
The face is smeared with powder and paste —
Enough to fool a fool,
Not one who seeks enlightenment.

The hair is curled in eight rings,
The eyes are rubbed with collyrium and rouge —
Enough to fool a fool,
Not one who seeks enlightenment.

Like a newly painted medicine box,
This foul body is adorned —
Enough to fool a fool,
Not one who seeks enlightenment.

The hunter set his trap and snare —
The wise deer does not touch the snare.
Having eaten the bait, we go away,
Leaving the trapper to mourn.

The hunter’s trap is cut —
The wise deer does not touch the snare.
Having eaten the bait, we go away,
Leaving the trapper to grieve.

From: Theragāthā 16.4
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, this saṃsāra is without discoverable beginning. A first point cannot be discerned of beings wandering and roaming on, shrouded by ignorance and bound by…
item.description:
“Monks, this saṃsāra is without discoverable beginning. A first point cannot be discerned of beings wandering and roaming on, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving. Monks, suppose a dog was tied by a leash to a strong post or pillar. If it walks, it walks close to that post or pillar. If it stands, it stands close to that post or pillar. If it sits, it sits close to that post or pillar. If it lies down, it lies down close to that post or pillar. So too, monks, an untaught ordinary person regards form as: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self.’ He regards feeling … perception … formations … consciousness as: ‘This is mine, this I am, this is my self.’ If he walks, he walks close to these five aggregates of clinging. If he stands, he stands close to them. If he sits, he sits close to them. If he lies down, he lies close to them. Therefore, monks, one should constantly reflect on one’s own mind thus: ‘For a long time, this mind has been defiled by greed, hatred, and delusion. Through the defilement of the mind, beings are defiled. Through the purification of the mind, beings are purified.’”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.100
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Mahā-Moggallāna: With hut whose roof is made of bones, Sewn together with sinews and flesh — Foul, full of stench! Carrying other people’s limbs…
item.description:
Elder Mahā-Moggallāna:

With hut whose roof is made of bones,
Sewn together with sinews and flesh —
Foul, full of stench!
Carrying other people’s limbs
As my own body!

The container full of feces,
Covered over with skin —
O female demon!
With your breasts like boils —
Your body has nine streams,
Always flowing.

Your body’s nine streams,
Foul, creating bondage —
The monk avoids that body
As a clean person avoids feces.

If others knew you
As I know you,
They would avoid you
As one avoids feces in the rain.

It is truly so, O Great Hero,
As the ascetic said:
Here, some are stuck,
Like an old ox in the mud.

One who thinks he can dye
The sky with turmeric,
Or with any other color —
That one only fails.

My mind is like that sky —
Well concentrated internally —
Do not bring evil thoughts to it,
Like a bird falling into a pile of fire.

From: Theragāthā 20.1
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Furthermore, monks, one might see that woman — sick, suffering, gravely ill, lying in her own urine and feces, needing others to lift her, needing…
item.description:
“Furthermore, monks, one might see that woman — sick, suffering, gravely ill, lying in her own urine and feces, needing others to lift her, needing others to lay her down. What do you think, monks? Has her former charm and beauty disappeared and peril become evident?” “Yes, venerable sir.” “Thus, monks, is the peril in the case of forms.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 13 – Mahādukkhakkhandhasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, I will teach you nine things rooted in craving. Listen and pay close attention. And what, monks, are the nine things rooted in craving?…
item.description:
“Monks, I will teach you nine things rooted in craving. Listen and pay close attention. And what, monks, are the nine things rooted in craving? Dependent on craving, seeking arises; dependent on seeking, gain arises; dependent on gain, decision arises; dependent on decision, desire and lust arise; dependent on desire and lust, attachment arises; dependent on attachment, possessiveness arises; dependent on possessiveness, guarding arises; dependent on guarding, the taking up of sticks and swords, quarrels, disputes, arguments, slander, false speech, and many evil, unwholesome states arise. These, monks, are the nine things rooted in craving.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 9.23
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Hearing the Buddha’s words, The wise monk fully understands the body, Sees it as it really is. As it is below, so above, As above,…
item.description:
“Hearing the Buddha’s words,
The wise monk fully understands the body,
Sees it as it really is.

As it is below, so above,
As above, so below —
Abandoning desire for the body inside and out,

The wise monk abandons desire and lust.
He attains the deathless, peace,
The constant state of Nibbāna.

This two-footed, impure, foul-smelling body
Filled with various rotting corpses, oozing —
Yet it is preserved and honored.

Who would think to exalt this body,
Or despise another —
Except one who is blind?”

From: Sutta Nipāta 1.11
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline, as intimacy with unwholesome states and…
item.description:
“I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline, as intimacy with unwholesome states and non-intimacy with wholesome states. Through intimacy with unwholesome states and non-intimacy with wholesome states, monks, unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 1.72
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Knowing this body to be like a clay pot, Fortifying this mind like a city, Fight Māra with the sword of wisdom, Guard your conquest,…
item.description:
“Knowing this body to be like a clay pot,
Fortifying this mind like a city,
Fight Māra with the sword of wisdom,
Guard your conquest, and be free of attachment.”

“Before long, alas, this body
Will lie on the ground,
Cast aside, devoid of consciousness,
Like a useless log.”

From: Dhammapada 33-43
📖 Read this sutta
